candidates.reasoning: The arid, scrubby terrain and reddish-brown soil are highly characteristic of the Spanish interior (Meseta) or southern highlands. The vegetation and road style are consistent with rural Castilla-La Mancha or Andalusia.
The landscape is very similar to the Alentejo or interior regions of Portugal, sharing the same Mediterranean climate and dry, rocky soil as Spain.
The rocky, sparsely vegetated hills and sun-bleached landscape are common in the Greek mainland, though the road quality and utility pole style are slightly more typical of Iberia.
The central Anatolian plateau features similar semi-arid terrain and rolling hills with sparse vegetation and similar soil coloration.

The arid, scrubby landscape with sparse, low-lying sclerophyllous vegetation and isolated hardy pines is highly characteristic of the Spanish Meseta or interior highlands. This specific combination of dry grassland and rocky scrub is common across central and southern Spain.

The vegetation is consistent with the interior Alentejo or Beira regions, which share the same Mediterranean scrubland and arid soil profiles as eastern Spain. It is botanically indistinguishable from the Spanish interior in this specific view.

The sparse, xeric vegetation and rocky terrain are similar to the mountainous interior of Greece. However, the specific density and type of scrub are slightly more indicative of the Iberian Peninsula.

The road features white outer edge lines and a broken white center line, which is standard for rural roads in Spain. The utility pole design is common in Mediterranean regions, specifically the Iberian Peninsula.
The infrastructure matches Greek rural road standards, including the white center line and edge markings. The arid landscape and pole style are consistent with Greek regional roads.
Turkey utilizes similar white lane markings and road construction standards for rural highways. The utility pole configuration is frequently seen across the Anatolian plateau.

The wooden utility pole with a simple cross-arm and the specific style of the thin white delineator post on the left are very common in rural Spanish highlands. This combination is distinct from similar poles in France or Italy.
The utility pole style and the arid environment are consistent with rural Greece, though the delineator post is slightly more characteristic of the Iberian Peninsula.
